\section{Introduction}

\outline{
\begin{itemize}
  \item briefly introduce archaeogaming as a concept
  \item introduce the state of archaeogaming as a field
  \item introduce the tools needed for archaeogaming
\end{itemize}
}

\textit{Archaeogaming} is a relatively recent field focusing on the study of games under the lens of archaeology~\cite{reinhard2018archaeogaming}.
One sub-focus of archaeogaming is the study of video games, and a further focus of that is the study of video games hand-written in assembly (i.e.\ before the advent of compilers).
For example, Aycock and Biittner demonstrate in \cite{aycock2024experimental} applied archaeogaming in the context of developing software using the Apple II for the Commodore 64.

This sub-focus heavily overlaps with reverse engineering: when you're given a binary, how do you approach understanding it?
The two major classes of analysis in this case are static analysis and dynamic analysis.
In static analysis, a researcher takes an external view of the binary as a whole, such as loading the binary into a disassembler such as Ghidra or IDA\@.
In dynamic analysis, the researcher is often running the program (either on hardware or via an emulator) using a debugger or some other ``live'' interface and are able to see the state of the machine along with the code it's running.

Static analysis faces major issues for the kinds of platforms our sub-focus is interested in.
First and foremost, the majority of static analysis software is purpose-built for code running on (relatively) modern hardware, where memory mapping is well-understood and mostly managed automatically by the underlying device.
This excludes our systems that have memory regions and banks for which the mapper chips themselves live in external hardware and are controlled via memory accesses rather than system calls.
Second, many static analysis techniques assume a binary format like ELF or PE, which have concepts of ``sections'' with different memory protections, whereas our code of interest being hand-written means instructions and data were very commonly interleaved throughout a binary.
However, the major issue that static analysis faces in general even outside of this sub-focus, is that a reverse engineer has no idea of the code's true execution paths.
When one looks at a single code path in a static analysis tool, there is no indication of what values might be in memory, how likely a function is to return a certain value, or how long a specific loop may run for.

Dynamic analysis solves some of these issues, but has issues of its own.
With dynamic analysis, one can tell what is being executed versus what is being loaded into registers to distinguish code from data.
One can also easily track many executions to determine common values and execution paths, solving the major issue of static analysis.
However, with dynamic analysis, it's very hard to get a full-program view: you might not yet know where a specific function is called from, and dynamic analysis can't tell you unless you know how to manipulate the program from the current state to call it.
This can even be impossible in some cases, for example if the function is called early in the program and you've already moved your debugger past that point.

There is a form of analysis that combines both the ``full context'' benefits of static analysis with the ``execution context'' benefits of dynamic analysis: trace analysis.
In trace analysis, the program is run once while collecting everything a debugger might ever output (instructions decoded, memory operations performed, etc), and this collection is output somewhere permanent.
Then, because this trace is effectively static (it was collected a single time and does not change from that point onward), it can aid the ability of static analysis.

\section{Motivation}

\outline{
\begin{itemize}
  \item motivate the need for tooling
  \item describe the current state of tooling
  \item motivate the new tool / research topic
\end{itemize}
}

Archaeogaming being a relatively new field means it is lacking in the maturity in resources and maturity in tooling that other fields have.
For the reverse engineering overlap mentioned previously, modern contexts are equipped with plenty of tools like GDB, IDA, and Ghidra, which have been in development for decades.
These tools aren't suited towards archaeogaming due to their focus on relatively modern environments: as mentioned before, the systems we are interested in break the common conventions of those that the mentioned tools are built to analyze.
This currently makes analyzing single games, not to mention a whole corpus of games together as one artifact, a relative challenge.

We take this as evidence of a need for new tools.
Specifically, a tool that allows the aggregation and analysis of a corpus of traces would answer many of these challenges.
One could iterate through individual states looking for certain conditions or find patterns among the whole dataset, among other analyses that may be useful for archaeogaming.
Some example usages for this tool (not exhaustive) may include:

\begin{description}
    \item[Single-Game Reverse Engineering] in which a user combines the SQL-like interface with a debugger in order to rewind and replay a specific section of code for the purpose of understanding that code segment.
    In modern settings, code is usually generated by a compiler, which makes the development of decompilers (such as those featured in IDA and Ghidra) an obvious solution.
    However, in our situation, the underlying binaries are effectively hand-written (having been assembled rather than compiled), and the architectures themselves make a representation in C much more difficult.
    \item[System Analysis] in which a user analyzes many traces over different games for the same system, looking for commonalities.
    For example, a user might want to analyze many different NES games and look for common behaviors.
    They might uncover a pattern of execution across games of ``wait for the zero-sprite interrupt, then change the scroll register'', which was a common method at the time of separating a HUD from a side-scrolling tile layer.
\end{description}

\section{Related Work}

\cite{bao2008hmtt} specifically focuses on platform-independent tracing.
As archaeogaming deals with a multitude of systems, the ideas from this work are relevant towards building an archaeogaming-focused system.

\cite{barr2014tardis} is about queries in the context of time-travel debugging, specifically at the implementation level.
Given the relationship between time-travel debugging and program traces, this work will be helpful for implementing a trace query system.

\cite{wang2011inferring} and \cite{walkinshaw2013inferring} detail approaches to inferring state patterns from execution traces.
Specifically \cite{wang2011inferring} is about network protocols, but the ideas and methods are applicable to more general state machines.
In games, there are often many different state machines that run concurrently, and it is important to be able to focus on how and where a single one of those machines is transformed.

\cite{kouame2015flexible} and \cite{wininger2017declarative} cover scripting and analysis for traces.
The main issue they tackle is the size of traces compared to the relative infrequency of useful data for a certain query.
This is very relevant in archaeogaming: as mentioned before, usually games have a lot of state machines running concurrently, so focusing on one state machine ends up discarding all other state machines between updates.

\cite{phang2013expositor} and \cite{willembrinck2021time} cover queries that make use of time-travel debugging.
These works will give insight on how to combine static analysis with debugging, as well as useful queries in other software that may be applicable to games.

\section{Methodology}\label{sec:methodology}

\outline{
\begin{itemize}
    \item start with a ``stupid'' analyzer in Python
    \item move to a ``less stupid'' SQL interface that uses a forward and backward copy of deltas
    \item move to a ``smart'' interface based on the ideas from PANDA
\end{itemize}
}

We may initially build such a tool as a prototype (i.e.\ in Python, with unoptimized logic) that scans traces generated by \textsc{Lava}.
\textsc{Lava} is an as-of-yet unpublished work, but an example of the trace format is shown in~\cref{fig:lava-snippet}.
As uncompressed traces can measure up to multiple gigabytes per minute, and scanning through them as files must be done linearly, this tool will be incredibly slow.
We will treat it as a baseline: the minimum required to be ``a tool that analyzes traces''.

    \centering\resizebox{0.4\textwidth}{!}{\theverbbox}
    \caption{A snippet of a \textsc{Lava} trace. The \texttt{R} commands show memory reads, \texttt{W} writes, \texttt{D} disassembly, and \texttt{I} logging from the tracer.}
    \label{fig:lava-snippet}
\end{figure}

Considering we want an SQL-like interface as the frontend for developer familiarity, we may consider moving the approach towards actual usage of a SQL database.
We could compare the storage of traces singularly versus the storage of a forward trace and a reversed trace for faster or more efficient rewinding.
There are existing works regarding the storage of program traces in databases (\cite{andjelkovic2011trace},~\cite{montplaisir2013efficient},~\cite{dolan2015repeatable}) which will be important references for efficient storage of our traces.
It is likely incorporating ideas from these works will lead to further performance gains and insights on possible queries.
Specifically, \cite{osborn2015playspecs} and \cite{goldsmith2005relational} detail the kinds of queries a programmer may want to make over state machines and program traces, respectively.

We will use the tool itself to perform case studies on various reverse engineering tasks in the context of archaeogaming.
We will document an example usage timeline of the tool in the process of single-game reverse engineering for a select few games, as well as an example usage for a corpus analysis.
These case studies will provide demonstrations of the chosen query language, and offer opportunities to compare time and resource costs with other tools and processes.

\section{Evaluation}

\outline{
\begin{itemize}
    \item time, space
    \item autoethnography, autobiographical design
\end{itemize}
}

We will evaluate success from two angles.
The first is the performance angle: how long does our tool take to run certain tasks, and how much space does it use?
Comparisons will be provided internally between different iterations of the tool, as well as other set-ups for specific case studies (refer to \cref{sec:methodology} for details on both).

The second angle is the utility of the tool in the archaeogaming context: it is worth noting that not everyone interested in archaeogaming has a strong computer science background.
We aim for the tool to be not only intuitive to use but also intuitive to understand.
Evaluating this metric is much harder, but there are well-documented methods for doing so in the field of HCI\@.

We will choose to take a path of autobiographical design, wherein we will document the usage process of our own design and analyze it via that documentation.
Taking autobiographical design into account has been shown ``to uncover detailed, subtle understandings that \ldots{} likely wouldn't have [been] found with other user-centered design techniques''~\cite{neustaedter2012autobiographical}.
As we who will be working on this project are predominantly video game reverse engineers, with skills in writing assembly for these older systems, we strongly believe that autobiographical design is the correct approach for evaluating the system we've built.

\section{Expectations}

\outline{
\begin{itemize}
    \item expecting to match the results of other reverse engineering works
    \item a new tool that enables analysis of systems and common practices
\end{itemize}
}

We expect to be able to match the results of other single-shot reverse engineering works, without a running copy of the program.
That is, we should be able to perform at least the same kinds of analysis using N traces with our tool as a regular reverse engineer would be able to perform with N live runs (without pokes).
This would be especially helpful to validate the use of traces themselves over live debugging for reverse engineering in the archaeogaming context.
We note that usage of the MAME debugger to record traces themselves is particularly slow, and direct time-travel is impossible by default in MAME\@.

We aim for this tool to fill a major part of the previously-discussed gap in archaeogaming tooling.
It is expected that this tool will be used in future archaeogaming works, and will be used in a broader reverse engineering context.
Among the other examples mentioned in~\cite{reinhard2018archaeogaming}, examples of analyses that may be performed using this tool include:
\begin{itemize}
    \item of old game programming patterns for which the exact reasoning or technique may have been lost to time
    \item of games that may have had development histories intertwined with time-specific cultural phenomena
    \item of how programming techniques have evolved across languages or borders (regional programming styles)
\end{itemize}
